title: Flat felt making methods
subtitle: A guide to the three principal approaches for making flat felt at home
excerpt: Flat felt can be made by hand-rubbing on a mat, by rolling around a tube, or by fulling in a washing machine. Each method produces a different surface quality and suits different projects. This reading explains the mechanics of each approach, when to choose one over another, and how the felt qualities differ.
sourceNotes: Comparative felting method guides are standard content in textile guild education. The mechanical principles draw on general wet-felting instruction across UK and Australian guild traditions.

content.text: All three methods use the same basic process. Hot water opens the wool fibres, soap helps the water soak through, and physical movement locks the fibres together. The methods differ in how that movement is applied, which changes the surface quality, the thickness, and how much control you have over the final size.
Method 1: Hand-rubbing on a bamboo mat
The roving is laid on a bamboo mat, covered with a felting net, wetted out, and rubbed by hand in circular strokes through the net for the initial locking phase, then directly on the surface for

. This is the highest-control method. The maker sees and feels the felt developing at every stage and can stop at exactly the right point. The tradeoff is time: a 50 x 50 cm panel takes 30-45 minutes of active rubbing. Hand-rubbing gives the most even surface but is physically demanding for large pieces.

After the initial locking phase, the felt is rolled tightly around a pool noodle, foam roller, or section of PVC pipe and wrapped in the bamboo mat. The whole bundle is rolled back and forth across a table or floor for repeated passes. Each session shrinks the felt in one direction; the piece is then unrolled, rotated 90 degrees, and re-rolled for the next session. This method allows much larger pieces than hand-rubbing alone and is less physically demanding per unit of time, but requires more space and gives the maker less continuous feedback.

Pre-felted cloth (partially felted by hand to the point where the fibres hold together but have not shrunk significantly) can be finished in a washing machine on a 30-degree wool cycle without spin. The machine agitation does the fulling work. This method is fast and suits yardage production: a 1-metre flat felt panel can be machine-fulled in two or three 30-degree cycles. The risks are pilling, uneven shrinkage, and irreversibility: unlike hand methods, there is no checking the felt mid-cycle. Machine fulling also uses more water and energy than hand methods.

For fine work such as cobweb felt, nuno felt, or any piece where surface quality is the primary criterion, hand-rubbing is the right choice. For larger functional pieces (bags, hats, vessels, panels over 50 cm) where the internal structure matters more than surface refinement, rolling is efficient. Machine fulling suits experienced makers producing yardage who have already confirmed their shrinkage rate through sampling.

All methods shrink the felt. The rate depends on the fibre (fine merino felts faster and shrinks more than Corriedale), the water temperature, and the duration of agitation. A rough guide: expect 20-35% linear shrinkage. A layout that starts at 100 x 100 cm will finish somewhere between 65 x 65 cm and 80 x 80 cm. Always make a test sample in the same fibre before cutting into a large project.
